name: Unnamed Area (Yule Marble)
short_description: BLM land near Yule Marble deposits offering public rockhounding opportunities for marble specimens and quartz minerals. Gentle slopes at 7,458 ft elevation in Colorado's scenic Pitkin County.
description:
This unnamed BLM area sits within the Yule Marble region of Pitkin County, offering recreational rockhounders access to one of North America's most famous marble formations. The Yule Marble quarries have supplied premium marble for decades, and dispersed fragments and specimens wash downslope into accessible public land. The gentle terrain and moderate elevation make this a practical site for collectors seeking marble samples, quartz veins, and feldspar-bearing rocks without the need for permits or fees.

The landscape consists of rolling mountainous terrain typical of the Elk Mountains, with scattered outcropping and loose material from natural erosion and historical quarrying activity. Marble here ranges from pure white to grey-veined specimens, often found as fragments in washes and along hillsides where gravity has naturally concentrated collectible material. The accessible positioning of this BLM parcel relative to the productive marble belt makes it attractive for day trips from Aspen or nearby towns.

Rockhounders should expect to hike moderate distances across open slopes and creek drainages where marble fragments accumulate. The site rewards patience and systematic searching along natural water drainage patterns. Early season visits before heavy summer growth may reveal fresh specimens exposed by spring runoff.
history: The Yule Marble deposit formed during the Proterozoic era through metamorphism of limestone beds in the ancestral Rocky Mountain uplift, creating some of the purest marble in North America. Intensive quarrying from the 1800s through present day has exposed vast marble bodies, and natural erosion continues to shed collectable fragments downslope into accessible public lands.
tips: Search creek bottoms and wash areas where marble naturally concentrates due to water transport and slope runoff - white or grey-veined pieces often cluster near drainage channels. Use a hand lens to examine feldspar content in larger specimens, and look for translucent quartz veins cutting through marble bodies. Carry a canvas bag and limit yourself to 25 lbs of the finest specimens rather than collecting everything, as marble is dense and weight adds up quickly. Early morning light on east-facing slopes makes white marble fragments more visible against soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7,458 feet with gentle to moderate slopes typical of the Elk Mountains. Open to lightly forested terrain with sagebrush and scattered conifers. Washes and creek drainages provide natural pathways for hiking. Footing is generally stable on exposed marble bedrock but loose rock and talus are common in drainage areas. Most productive collecting areas are within 0.5 to 2 miles of parking on foot.
written_directions: From Pitkin, head northeast on Colorado State Highway 82 toward Aspen for approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access roads or pull-outs on the east or west side of the highway in areas marked as public land (check BLM.gov maps prior to visit). GPS coordinates 39.122698, -107.26985 mark the general area - use detailed topographic maps or Google Earth to identify parking access points and legal entry to public land parcels. Do not cross private property or posted mineral claims.
road_conditions: Approach via paved State Highway 82. Final access roads may be dirt or gravel with moderate clearance. Confirm current conditions with Aspen BLM office.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along State Highway 82 or designated BLM access areas. No developed parking lot; expect roadside or primitive parking for 2-4 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or powered tools. Respect all posted mineral claims and private property boundaries. Leave no disturbance behind.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No powered tools
Respect posted mineral claims
Do not enter private property
nearest_services: Pitkin, approximately 8 miles southwest, has limited services. Aspen, approximately 12 miles south, offers full services including gas, food, water, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven slopes and loose rock common in washes. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots recommended due to marble scree and variable footing. Hiking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles from parking are typical. Elevation gain modest but steady. Site suitable for families with school-age children and dogs with good footing.

faq.question: What types of marble can I find rockhounding at the Yule Marble area near Pitkin, Colorado?
faq.answer: The Yule Marble BLM area yields white, grey-veined, and translucent marble specimens ranging from small hand samples to larger chunks. You may also find quartz veins and feldspar-bearing samples cutting through marble bodies. Marble color generally runs from pure white to light grey with distinctive veining patterns from the original limestone metamorphism.

faq.question: Is a permit required to collect at this unnamed BLM rockhounding site in Colorado?
faq.answer: No permit is required. This is public BLM land open to recreational personal-use collection. You simply need to follow standard BLM rules: 25 lbs per day limit, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation. Always verify current access via BLM.gov before visiting.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound for marble specimens in the Pitkin County area?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal, when high elevation access (7,458 ft) is clear of snow and weather is stable. Spring is promising too as snowmelt feeds washes with fresh marble fragments, but roads may not be passable until early June. Avoid winter when heavy snow closes mountain access.

faq.question: How difficult is hiking and terrain at the Yule Marble rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Terrain is moderate. Expect gentle to rolling slopes with some loose rock and marble scree in washes. Most productive areas are within 2 miles of parking on foot. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to marble talus and variable footing. The site is accessible to families and dogs with reasonable fitness levels.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding marble near Aspen?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens to examine feldspar and quartz details. A canvas or cloth bag works better than hard containers for marble samples since weight adds up fast. Sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, water, and a topographic map or GPS unit are essential. A small chisel may help extract specimens, but avoid motorized tools which are prohibited on BLM land.

faq.question: Is this Pitkin County marble rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is beginner-friendly and family-appropriate. The public BLM access requires no permits, gentle terrain is accessible to children 8 and older, and marble specimens are abundant and easy to identify. The 25 lb daily limit prevents overexertion. Nearby services in Aspen and Pitkin make it easy to plan a day trip.

meta_description: Find marble, quartz, and feldspar on BLM land near Pitkin, Colorado. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly high-elevation rockhounding.
